-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/starlette-1.0.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/starlette-1.0.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/starlette-1.0.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/pip-26.0.1.dist-info/licenses/src/pip/_vendor/idna/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/starlette-1.0.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/starlette-1.0.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/starlette-1.0.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/starlette-1.0.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/starlette-1.0.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/starlette-1.0.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|LICENSE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/venv/Lib/site-packages/uvicorn-0.46.0.dist-info/licenses/LICENSE|license]] by Nous Research, represents a critical evolution in agentic design. Characterized by a closed learning loop, autonomous procedural [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] creation, and deep Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration, Hermes provides an infrastructure capable of continuous self-improvement. As of May 2026, the framework has achieved massive adoption, recently surpassing 140,000 GitHub stars, solidifying its position as the premier framework for always-on, locally executed or cloud-deployed autonomous [[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|agents]].   

For an autonomous enterprise system such as Keystone Sovereign—which is tasked with simultaneously orchestrating a physical construction business, managing a portfolio of YouTube media channels, and operating a vast health content empire—the Hermes framework provides a highly decoupled, infinitely extensible foundation. Traditional monolithic [[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|agents]] fail in multi-domain scenarios due to context window degradation, instruction overload, and brittle API integrations. Hermes circumvents these limitations through a dual-memory system. Semantic knowledge is persisted across sessions via FTS5 full-text search SQLite databases and Honcho dialectic user modeling, while procedural knowledge is autonomously synthesized into isolated, on-demand "Skills". This report provides an exhaustive technical blueprint for implementing autonomous [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] creation and dynamic tool discovery patterns within the Hermes Agent ecosystem, tailored specifically for the Keystone Sovereign tri-domain architecture.   

Architectural Foundations and the Autonomous Learning Loop

Traditional artificial intelligence [[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|agents]] suffer from context amnesia. They require identical, verbose instructions for repeated tasks, consuming immense token budgets and demanding continuous human oversight. Hermes mitigates this through an architecture designed entirely around a built-in learning loop. When Hermes successfully navigates a complex, multi-step problem, it does not discard the execution trace. Instead, it utilizes its internal toolsets to distill the solution path into a reusable procedural document, persisting this knowledge for future sessions.   

This closed learning loop is the defining characteristic of the system. It consists of agent-curated memory with periodic self-nudges, autonomous [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] creation, [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] self-improvement during use, and cross-session recall with LLM summarization. When Keystone Sovereign encounters a novel workflow—such as aggregating specific biomedical studies, formatting them into a comparative script, and triggering a visual rendering pipeline—the agent operates initially in a high-token exploration phase. Once the successful sequence of tool calls is identified, the system instructions prompt the agent to formalize the routine.   

The formalization of this procedural knowledge is not stored as a transient database entry, but rather written directly to the local filesystem in the ~/.hermes/skills/ directory as a Markdown-based knowledge document. This separation of concerns ensures that the core MEMORY.md and USER.md files—which are strictly bounded to approximately 2,200 characters and 1,375 characters respectively—are reserved purely for environmental facts and user preferences, preventing semantic memory from being polluted by lengthy procedural instructions.   

Autonomous [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|Skill]] Creation Protocol

Skills in the Hermes framework are not compiled binaries or raw Python scripts. They are highly structured Markdown files ([[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|SKILL]].md) that combine natural language instructions, invocation triggers, prerequisite validation, and explicit fallback procedures. The agent autonomously writes, refines, and manages these files using the specialized skill_manage toolset.   

The Mechanics of the [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|Skill]] Management Toolset

The skill_manage tool provides the agent with granular control over its own procedural memory through several distinct actions. By utilizing these actions, Hermes bypasses the need for human developers to manually update its capabilities.   

Action Parameter	Operational Purpose	Key Required Parameters
create	Synthesizes a new [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] from scratch after a successful novel workflow execution.	name, content (the full [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|SKILL]].md payload), optional category.
patch	Applies targeted, token-efficient fixes to existing skills using string replacement.	name, old_string, new_string.
edit	Executes major structural rewrites of a [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] document.	name, content (full [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|SKILL]].md replacement).
delete	Removes a [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] entirely if it is deemed deprecated or redundant.	name.
write_file	Adds or updates supporting scripts and templates within the [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]]'s subdirectory.	name, file_path, file_content.
remove_file	Deletes a supporting file from the [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]]'s subdirectory.	name, file_path.

The distinction between the edit and patch actions is particularly vital for the economic operation of the agent. The patch action is the strongly preferred mechanism for autonomous updates. When a [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] requires a minor adjustment—such as updating an API endpoint or refining a prompt directive—rewriting the entire document via edit consumes a massive amount of output tokens. Instead, the agent executes a patch, targeting only the specific string that needs modification, drastically optimizing inference costs during the self-improvement phase.   

Furthermore, the write_file action allows a [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] to be more than just a single Markdown document. A [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] directory can contain a scripts/ folder for complex parsing logic or a references/ folder for contextual data, which the agent can autonomously populate and execute during the workflow.   

Progressive Disclosure and Token Economics

A foundational challenge in agentic architecture is context window management. If Keystone Sovereign possesses hundreds of specialized skills across construction, media, and healthcare, injecting all of them into the system prompt simultaneously would instantly exhaust the model's context capacity and degrade reasoning quality. Hermes solves this through an architectural pattern known as "Progressive Disclosure".   

Under the progressive disclosure model, skills are treated as on-demand knowledge documents that the agent selectively loads. The sequence operates with strict token efficiency. At the initiation of a session, the system automatically executes skills_list(), which injects a highly compressed manifest into the context window. This manifest consumes only about 3,000 tokens and contains nothing more than the name and a strictly truncated description (capped at 60 characters) of every available [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] on the filesystem.   

When the agent's internal reasoning engine determines that a specific procedure is required to fulfill a user request, it autonomously executes the skill_view(name) tool. This action fetches the complete contents of the requested [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|SKILL]].md file. Crucially, recent architectural updates to Hermes have altered how this content is handled. Instead of dumping the massive text block into the transient conversation transcript—which would result in severe context duplication if the [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] is referenced multiple times—the system dynamically injects the content into a dedicated "active [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] block" registry within the prompt builder. This ensures stable hashing, prevents prompt-cache locality damage, and allows large skills to act as persistent, deduped context attachments. The agent also possesses the capability to load specific supporting files granularly using skill_view(name, file_path) if the primary [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|SKILL]].md dictates that external scripts are necessary.   

The Architecture of a [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|SKILL]].md Document

To ensure that autonomously created skills function deterministically within isolated sandboxes, the [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|SKILL]].md format adheres to a strict schema. The document is bifurcated into a YAML frontmatter block for configuration and a standard Markdown body for the procedural instructions.   

The frontmatter defines the metadata, hardware requirements, and conditional activation parameters.   

Frontmatter Key	Technical Description and Implication
name	The unique identifier for the [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]], used for invocation via the CLI (/name) or internal tool calling.
platforms	Restricts execution to specific operating systems (e.g., [macos, linux]). Omission allows universal loading.
requires_toolsets	A strict validation check. The [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] is completely hidden from skills_list if the specified toolsets (e.g., web, browser) are not currently active in the session configuration.
requires_tools	A granular validation check requiring specific individual tools to be present before the [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] is loaded.
fallback_for_tools	An inverse trigger. The [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] is intentionally hidden if the specified tools are available, allowing the [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] to act as a fallback procedure when primary capabilities are offline.
required_environment_variables	Declares critical secrets (e.g., FAL_KEY). If present, these are automatically and securely passed through to Docker or Modal sandboxes without leaking into the LLM context.
required_credential_files	Specifies paths to OAuth tokens or credential JSONs that must be dynamically bind-mounted into remote execution sandboxes.

The body of the document contains the actual operational logic. A properly formatted [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]], whether authored by a human engineer or autonomously generated by the agent, follows a specific header hierarchy: # [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|Skill]] Title, ## When to Use (trigger conditions), ## Quick Reference, ## Procedure (the step-by-step logic), ## Pitfalls (known failure modes), and ## Verification (instructions for validating success).   

During execution, Hermes provides runtime template tokens that can be substituted into the [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]]'s instructions. For example, utilizing ${HERMES_SKILL_DIR} allows the [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] to dynamically resolve the absolute path to its own supporting scripts directory, regardless of where the agent is deployed, while ${HERMES_SESSION_ID} injects the active session identifier for logging and cross-reference purposes.   

Designing Foundational Skills for Keystone Sovereign

While the system is designed to create skills autonomously, an enterprise deployment requires seeding foundational skills that wrap complex APIs and specialized external processes. A critical distinction must be made during architectural planning: when to build a native Python "Tool" versus when to construct a Markdown "[[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|Skill]]."

The official heuristic dictates that an integration should be a [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|Skill]] when the capability can be expressed purely as a sequence of existing tools, shell commands, and API interactions. A native Tool is only required when the integration involves binary data handling, real-time event streaming, or complex internal authentication flows. For Keystone Sovereign, almost all operational logic should be codified as Skills, relying on the agent's core execute_code, terminal, and web_extract tools to execute the instructions.   

Subagent-Driven Development and Systematic Debugging

A major paradigm within the Hermes ecosystem is the delegation of tasks. The system ships with bundled skills such as software-development/subagent-driven-development and software-development/systematic-debugging. These skills leverage the delegate_task tool, which spawns isolated, short-lived subagents with distinct context windows.   

When Keystone Sovereign authors a complex Python script or modifies its own configurations, it does not verify its own work within the same context window. The subagent-driven-development [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] enforces a two-stage review process. The primary agent executes the implementation, then calls delegate_task to spawn a reviewer agent. This reviewer receives only the git diff and the static scan results, intentionally isolated from the implementer's reasoning context. This fail-closed mechanism prevents the primary agent from hallucinating success based on its own prior context.   

Media Pipeline Orchestration [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|Skill]]

For the YouTube media division of Keystone Sovereign, the generation of high-fidelity visual assets is a continuous requirement. Hermes integrates natively with FAL.ai, supporting advanced models like fal-ai/flux-2-pro for studio photorealism, fal-ai/ideogram/v3 for complex typography, and fal-ai/recraft/v4/pro for brand system generation. The system dynamically routes between text-to-image and image-to-video modalities using the consolidated video_generate tool; passing an image_url alongside the prompt animates a still image, while omitting it generates video purely from text.   

To operationalize this without forcing the agent to reason through the model selection and API parameters on every request, a foundational [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] must be implemented. Below is the technical specification for a youtube-content-generator [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]], illustrating the integration of environment variable passthrough and media delivery [[DIRECTIVES|directives]].

YAML
---
name: youtube-content-generator
description: Orchestrates FAL.ai image generation and compiles video assets for YouTube.
version: 1.2.0
author: Keystone Sovereign Architecture
platforms: [linux, macos]
metadata:
  hermes:
    tags:
    requires_tools: [video_generate, image_generate, execute_code]
required_environment_variables:
  - name: FAL_KEY
    help: "Required for high-resolution rendering via fal.ai"
---
# YouTube Content Generator

## When to Use
Trigger this [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] when requested to generate visual assets, thumbnails, or short-form video content for the health or construction YouTube channels.

## Procedure
1. **Determine Modality:** Identify if the request is for a static thumbnail (`image_generate`) or a B-roll sequence (`video_generate`).
2. **Select Model:** Use `fal-ai/flux-2-pro` for hyper-realistic construction site renderings. Use `fal-ai/ideogram/v3` for thumbnails requiring precise typography.
3. **Execute Generation:** Call the appropriate tool. For video, pass `image_url` to animate a still, or omit it to generate from text alone.
4. **Media Delivery:** Emit the exact directive `[[as_document]]` on the last line of your response to ensure the high-resolution file is delivered as a lossless attachment, rather than a transient, compressed chat image.

## Pitfalls
- If `video_generate` returns a timeout, fall back to `fal-ai/flux-2/klein/9b` for faster, lower-resolution proxy generation.


The inclusion of the [[as_document]] directive is a specific Hermes protocol. When the agent appends this tag to its response, the system bypasses standard UI compression, ensuring that the generated 4K media files are delivered losslessly to the user or downstream storage buckets. The FAL_KEY definition ensures that the API key is injected directly into the secure execution environment, isolating it from general logging and protecting the account balance.   

Tool Discovery Patterns: The Model Context Protocol (MCP)

While procedural logic is governed by skills, interacting with diverse, proprietary external software architectures requires a standardized communication layer. In the Hermes 2026 architecture, this is achieved entirely through the Model Context Protocol (MCP). MCP represents a paradigm shift; it completely removes the need to write native Python wrappers within the Hermes codebase to interact with third-party services.   

By configuring external MCP servers, Hermes dynamically discovers, registers, and executes tools provided by external hosts at startup. This creates a perfectly decoupled architecture where the construction estimation engine, the YouTube analytics dashboard, and the health literature databases operate independently from the core agent binary.   

MCP Transport Mechanisms and Configuration Protocols

Hermes reads its MCP configurations from the ~/.hermes/config.yaml file, specifically under the mcp_servers configuration block. The system supports two primary transport layers for these servers:   

Stdio Servers: These are local subprocesses that the Hermes engine spawns directly on the host machine. Communication occurs entirely over stdin and stdout. This transport is highly secure and offers negligible latency, making it ideal for servers that manipulate the local filesystem, interact with local Git repositories, or act as bridges to local command-line interfaces. Configuration requires the command, args, and env properties.   

HTTP/SSE Servers: These are remote endpoints that Hermes communicates with via standard URLs and HTTP headers. This transport is essential for connecting to organization-wide internal MCP endpoints, serverless functions, or direct integrations hosted by third-party SaaS providers. Configuration requires the url and headers (typically containing Authorization tokens) properties.   

Namespace Collision and Prefixing

In a complex multi-domain system like Keystone Sovereign, integrating dozens of MCP servers poses a massive risk of tool name collision. For instance, both a GitHub MCP server and a Procore MCP server might attempt to register a tool simply named create_issue.

Hermes automatically prevents this by enforcing a strict namespace protocol during the discovery phase at startup. Every tool discovered from an MCP server is prefixed with the server's configured name. The create_issue tool from a server named procore is automatically registered internally as mcp_procore_create_issue, while the GitHub tool becomes mcp_github_create_issue. This invisible routing layer ensures the LLM can precisely select the correct tool without requiring the user to memorize complex invocation paths.   

Security Filtering and Exposure Control

Connecting an autonomous LLM to enterprise infrastructure carries profound security implications. If a comprehensive Google [[.agents/rules/workspace|Workspace]] MCP server is attached without constraints, a hallucinating agent technically possesses the API surface area to recursively delete company drives.

Hermes addresses this threat through aggressive per-server filtering [[DIRECTIVES|directives]] applied directly in the config.yaml file. The tools.include parameter acts as a strict whitelist; only the specific tools enumerated in this array are exposed to the agent, while all other advertised tools are silently dropped during the MCP handshake. Conversely, tools.exclude operates as a blacklist. When both are defined, the include whitelist takes absolute precedence.   

Beyond standard executable tools, MCP servers can expose "Resources" (static data like blueprints or documentation) and "Prompts" (pre-configured instruction templates). Hermes automatically generates capability-aware utility wrappers for these, such as list_resources and get_prompt. To prevent the agent from unnecessarily exploring massive corporate wikis and wasting tokens, administrators can globally disable these utilities per server by setting tools.prompts: false and tools.resources: false.   

Parallel Execution and Timeout Management

By default, the execution of [[.agents/rules/mcp_tools|MCP tools]] is sequential, as many API endpoints do not tolerate concurrent modifications. However, for read-heavy operations, sequential execution creates severe bottlenecks. Hermes allows administrators to toggle supports_parallel_tool_calls: true in the configuration block. When enabled, the agent can dispatch multiple independent API queries (e.g., fetching five different Procore drawings) simultaneously within a single tool-call batch, drastically reducing overall latency.   

Execution reliability is maintained via the timeout and connect_timeout parameters. If a remote HTTP MCP server crashes mid-request or hangs, Hermes relies on these hard limits to terminate the connection, preventing the agent's main thread from blocking indefinitely.   

Bridging Legacy Systems: FastMCP and OpenAPI Integration

While modern SaaS platforms increasingly offer native MCP endpoints, an enterprise architecture like Keystone Sovereign inevitably relies on legacy proprietary systems, highly customized ERPs, and specialized databases that expose traditional REST APIs. Building custom MCP servers from scratch to bridge these legacy APIs is a time-consuming and fragile process.

As of early 2026, the industry standard solution for this challenge is FastMCP (specifically v3.1.0), an Apache-2.0 licensed Python framework that has achieved dominant market share, powering approximately 70% of all MCP servers globally. The most powerful feature of FastMCP for enterprise architects is the FastMCP.from_openapi() class method.   

Dynamic Tool Generation via OpenAPI Spec Parsing

The from_openapi() method serves as an automated translation layer. Instead of manually coding Python wrappers for hundreds of REST endpoints, an engineer can instantiate a fully compliant MCP server by simply passing the legacy system's OpenAPI (Swagger) JSON specification to the method.   

Under the hood, FastMCP utilizes the OpenAPIProvider to parse the specification. It automatically converts API endpoints into [[.agents/rules/mcp_tools|MCP tools]], handling the complex translation of HTTP mechanics. The generation process includes sophisticated parameter handling:   

Name Generation: Endpoint operationId values are slugified and truncated to fit within the 56-character MCP limit. If naming collisions occur, numbers are automatically appended.   

Parameter Validation: FastMCP natively handles required parameter enforcement. If the LLM forgets a required path parameter, FastMCP intercepts the call and raises a clear error back to the agent before the HTTP request is even dispatched, preventing malformed requests from hitting the legacy server.   

Data Formatting: Array parameters are serialized correctly based on the OpenAPI explode parameter (e.g., formatting path arrays as comma-separated values), and empty query parameters are automatically stripped from the outgoing request.   

Advanced Route Mapping and Architectural Logic

By default, the from_openapi() engine maps every single discovered endpoint to an actionable MCP Tool. While functional, this is often structurally inappropriate for massive databases. For example, a GET endpoint designed to return a gigabyte of historical project drawings should not be an executable tool that attempts to dump its JSON payload directly into the LLM's limited context window.   

To address this, FastMCP provides the RouteMap mechanism, allowing engineers to systematically override the default translation logic based on HTTP methods, regex path patterns, or OpenAPI tags.   

Python
import httpx
from fastmcp import FastMCP
from fastmcp.server.providers.openapi import RouteMap, MCPType

# Define authenticated client for a custom legacy ERP
# Critical: Set specific User-Agent as some enterprise firewalls block default httpx signatures
auth_headers = {
    "Authorization": "Bearer ${ERP_TOKEN}", 
    "User-Agent": "Keystone-Sovereign-MCP-Bridge"
}
client = httpx.AsyncClient(base_url="https://api.keystone-erp.com", headers=auth_headers)

# Fetch the live OpenAPI specification JSON
spec = httpx.get("https://api.keystone-erp.com/swagger.json").json()

# Generate the MCP Server with customized routing logic
mcp = FastMCP.from_openapi(
    openapi_spec=spec,
    client=client,
    name="Keystone ERP Bridge",
    timeout=45.0, # Global timeout for all proxy requests
    route_maps=, 
            pattern=r"^/projects/\{id\}/blueprints/.*", 
            mcp_type=MCPType.RESOURCE_TEMPLATE
        ),
        # Explicitly exclude all destructive financial endpoints at the bridge layer
        RouteMap(
            methods=, 
            pattern=r"^/finance/.*", 
            mcp_type=MCPType.EXCLUDE
        ),
        # Final catch-all: Exclude everything not explicitly handled above (Strict Whitelist)
        RouteMap(mcp_type=MCPType.EXCLUDE)
    ]
)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    mcp.run(transport="stdio")


This Python script effectively acts as an impenetrable middleware layer. The MCPType.RESOURCE_TEMPLATE directive is particularly vital; it transforms a GET endpoint into a templated URI that Hermes can read natively, fetching data only when specifically required, rather than forcing the agent to execute a function call. The final RouteMap(mcp_type=MCPType.EXCLUDE) acts as a catch-all security net, ensuring that any endpoint not explicitly whitelisted is invisible to the agent. The timeout=45.0 parameter ensures that long-running database queries do not permanently hang the agent's reasoning loop. Furthermore, customizing the User-Agent header is a critical operational detail, as many enterprise Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) immediately drop traffic originating from default python-httpx signatures.   

Domain-Specific MCP Implementations for Keystone Sovereign

With the architectural foundation and bridging mechanics established, the system requires precise configuration to address the tri-domain operational requirements of Keystone Sovereign.

1. Construction Management: Procore and Deterministic Estimation

Managing a physical construction business requires real-time synchronization with project management software and flawless execution of mathematical estimations. For project management, organizations can leverage MCP wrappers around Pipedream and Zapier interfaces to interact with Procore, enabling the agent to autonomously execute actions such as Create RFI, Create Submittal, and Create Timesheet. For deep data analytics, the CData Python MCP server allows Hermes to query Procore datasets as if they were local relational databases.   

However, the most critical integration involves material estimation. Large Language Models are inherently stochastic and frequently hallucinate arithmetic, making them dangerously unreliable for calculating structural loads or material orders. To solve this, the architecture incorporates the viranjpatel-construction-mcp-server. This is an ultra-lightweight, deterministic MCP server specifically designed for construction calculations. By providing explicit tools for Material Calculator (concrete, steel, and brick volume requirements) and Cost Estimator (multiplying volumes by localized pricing tables), the LLM offloads the mathematics to a deterministic compiler. The agent reasons about what needs to be built, but the MCP server handles the exact dimensional arithmetic, ensuring structural integrity.   

2. Media Operations: Google [[.agents/rules/workspace|Workspace]] and Content Orchestration

Managing a portfolio of YouTube channels requires deep integration with corporate documentation and scheduling infrastructure. The standard implementation utilizes the taylorwilsdon/google_workspace_mcp server. Because this server requires access to highly sensitive corporate emails, documents, and calendars, it is instantiated locally via the stdio transport.   

The configuration requires pointing the GOOGLE_MCP_CREDENTIALS_DIR environment variable to a secure directory containing the OAuth credentials.json file. Furthermore, to control the massive surface area of Google [[.agents/rules/workspace|Workspace]], administrators can restrict the server's toolsets via environment variables or comma-separated command-line flags (e.g., X-MCP-Tools: "get_file_contents,issue_read") to strictly limit the agent to document drafting and spreadsheet analysis without granting email deletion privileges. Through this integration, the agent can autonomously read production schedules in Google Sheets, draft formatted video scripts into Google Docs, and monitor collaborative edits from human producers.   

3. The Health Content Empire: PubMed Literature Synthesis

The health content division relies on accurate, up-to-date biomedical literature. Rather than relying on fragile web scraping or hallucinated medical facts, Keystone Sovereign integrates the cyanheads/pubmed-mcp-server. This server connects directly to the NCBI E-utilities API, providing a suite of specialized biomedical research tools.   

The agent utilizes pubmed_search_articles to query the database with high precision, applying date ranges and field-specific filters to isolate recent clinical trials. It then employs the pubmed_fetch_fulltext tool, which attempts to retrieve the full article content. Crucially, this tool handles complex fallback logic; if a document is not available directly on PubMed Central, it automatically queries the Unpaywall API to locate open-access DOIs, converting HTML or PDF documents into clean Markdown for the agent's context window. Finally, when generating scripts or health articles, the agent calls pubmed_format_citations to instantly generate APA 7th or MLA 9th formatted reference lists, completely eliminating manual bibliographic work.   

Consolidated Multi-Domain Configuration

The diverse MCP requirements are consolidated within the core configuration file. The config.yaml snippet below illustrates the precise configuration pattern for Keystone Sovereign's triad:

YAML
mcp_servers:
  # Domain 1: Construction Management (Deterministic Math & ERP)
  construction_calculator:
    command: "npx"
    args: ["-y", "viranjpatel-construction-mcp-server"]
    enabled: true

  procore_bridge:
    command: "uvx"
    args: ["mcp-server-procore"]
    env:
      PROCORE_ACCESS_TOKEN: "${PROCORE_SECRET}"
    tools:
      include: [list_rfis, create_submittal, create_timesheet]
      prompts: false
      resources: false

  # Domain 2: Health Content Empire (Biomedical Research)
  pubmed_research:
    url: "https://pubmed.caseyjhand.com/mcp"
    connect_timeout: 30
    timeout: 120
    tools:
      include: [pubmed_search_articles, pubmed_fetch_fulltext, pubmed_format_citations]
      prompts: true
      resources: true

  # Domain 3: YouTube & Media Operations (Document Orchestration)
  google_workspace:
    command: "npx"
    args: ["-y", "@taylorwilsdon/google_workspace_mcp"]
    env:
      GOOGLE_MCP_CREDENTIALS_DIR: "/[[.agents/rules/workspace|workspace]]/secrets/gcp"
    supports_parallel_tool_calls: true


The use of ${PROCORE_SECRET} demonstrates the environment variable substitution syntax; Hermes resolves this at runtime, ensuring plaintext secrets are never committed to the YAML configuration. The pubmed_research block utilizes the HTTP transport, explicitly setting generous timeouts to accommodate large document downloads. The google_workspace block enables supports_parallel_tool_calls, allowing the agent to simultaneously read multiple spreadsheets to quickly assemble a production schedule.   

Security Architecture and Sandboxed Execution

The deployment of a fully autonomous agent managing production enterprise assets demands an aggressive, paranoid security posture. Hermes implements a defense-in-depth model comprising seven distinct architectural layers, ranging from User Authorization (allowlists and strict Telegram/Discord DM pairing) to Cross-Session Isolation (preventing directory traversal attacks across different conversation threads).   

For an architecture like Keystone Sovereign, where the agent is executing generated code, manipulating files, and calling external APIs autonomously 24/7, three specific security mechanisms are absolutely critical: Container Isolation, Pre-Execution Scanning, and Credential Filtering.

Container Isolation and Execution Backends

By default, permitting an AI agent to execute arbitrary shell commands on a bare-metal host is a catastrophic vulnerability. A hallucinating agent could easily overwrite system libraries or format drives. Hermes mitigates this entirely through its terminal backend abstraction layer. The configuration setting terminal.backend redirects all execute_code and terminal tool operations away from the host system and into isolated sandboxes.   

Supported backends include docker, daytona, modal, singularity, and vercel_sandbox. When configured for local Docker isolation (terminal.backend: docker), Hermes dynamically spawns an ephemeral container applying highly restrictive security parameters. These parameters represent a hardened security profile:   

--cap-drop ALL: This flag strips all Linux kernel capabilities from the container, enforcing the principle of least privilege.   

--pids-limit 256: This strictly limits the maximum number of running processes, preventing LLM-generated bash scripts from initiating fork bombs that could exhaust host hardware resources.   

--security-opt no-new-privileges: This directive blocks container processes from escalating their privileges via suid or sgid binaries.   

--tmpfs /tmp:rw,nosuid,size=512m: Temporary directories are mounted in memory with strict size limits, neutralizing storage-filling denial-of-service attacks.   

Because the container acts as an impenetrable boundary, destructive commands inside it cannot harm the host machine. Consequently, Hermes intentionally bypasses its internal interactive "Dangerous Command Approval" prompts when running in these backends. This bypass is a strict operational requirement; an autonomous agent cannot halt execution at 3:00 AM to ask a human administrator if it is permitted to execute a standard file deletion routine within a disposable, isolated Docker volume.   

For highly secured deployments utilizing NVIDIA enterprise hardware, Hermes can alternatively be deployed inside NVIDIA's NemoClaw OpenShell sandbox. This architecture provides extreme kernel-level enforcement. Landlock modules restrict filesystem writes to exactly three allowlisted directories, and strict Seccomp profiles block access to dangerous system calls. This ensures that even if an advanced prompt injection attack forces the LLM to write a complex C exploit, the underlying kernel simply denies execution.   

Pre-Execution Scanning with Tirith

While containers isolate the host from the agent, they do not protect the agent from malicious external inputs. An agent autonomously downloading a shared community [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] from GitHub or fetching an unverified MCP configuration is highly vulnerable to supply-chain attacks. To counter this, Hermes integrates Tirith (sheeki03/tirith), a locally executing, ultra-fast (sub-1 millisecond latency) security scanner.   

Tirith operates as a silent gatekeeper. Before any command is executed, any content is pasted into the terminal, or any file is scanned, Tirith analyzes the payload against a signed threat intelligence database. It operates entirely offline, ensuring no telemetry leaves the host machine. Tirith specifically targets complex attack vectors that easily deceive LLMs:   

Homograph Attacks: It detects lookalike domains (e.g., a Cyrillic 'і' replacing a Latin 'i') designed to trick the agent into downloading payloads from malicious mirrors.   

Terminal Injection: It identifies and neutralizes ANSI escape sequences and bidi overrides hidden in logs or web scrapes that attempt to hijack the terminal output.   

Pipe-to-Shell Patterns: It flags dangerous installation vectors such as curl | bash that attackers frequently embed in compromised repositories.   

Within the Hermes ecosystem, Tirith provides specialized security tools. The tirith_verify_mcp_config tool is executed to scan external MCP configurations, specifically looking for insecure HTTP endpoints, dangerous wildcard tool exposures, and subtle shell injection vulnerabilities hidden within the JSON args array. Furthermore, tirith_fetch_cloaking analyzes web endpoints to detect server-side cloaking—instances where a malicious server delivers benign content to human browsers but serves exploitation payloads to AI user-[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|AGENTS]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/AGENTS|agents]].   

MCP Credential Filtering and Secret Passthrough

When Hermes spawns a local stdio MCP server, it must pass environment variables to the subprocess (e.g., providing the GOOGLE_MCP_CREDENTIALS_DIR path). However, passing the entire host environment would recklessly expose every API key to the child process.

Hermes enforces strict Credential Filtering at Layer 4 of its security architecture. Before the subprocess is spawned, the environment is aggressively wiped. Only variables explicitly declared within the env block of the mcp_servers configuration, alongside a highly restricted safe baseline (like PATH), are passed to the executable.   

This security paradigm extends to the [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] system. If a [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|SKILL]].md file declares required environment variables (like FAL_KEY) in its YAML frontmatter, the env_passthrough system ensures those specific variables are securely injected into the remote Docker or Modal execution sandboxes. The keys remain completely hidden from the general chat transcript, logging systems, and the LLM's raw context window, ensuring that even a compromised model cannot exfiltrate the raw text of the API token.   

Operational Execution, Automation, and Maintenance

Operating Keystone Sovereign continuously requires orchestrating [[STATE|STATE]]|[[STATE|state]], handling API quotas efficiently, and directing autonomous workflows without manual intervention.

Persistent Memory and The Dialectic Model

Hermes manages long-term cross-session persistence through an FTS5 full-text SQLite database. This database is augmented by continuous summarization routines that distill conversational interactions into two foundational files: MEMORY.md (which stores environmental facts, project states, and operational contexts) and USER.md (which records operator preferences and constraints).   

The agent utilizes Honcho dialectic user modeling to refine its understanding of the environment over time. To maintain inference efficiency and prevent prompt bloat, this persistent memory is intentionally bounded. The MEMORY.md file is restricted to approximately 2,200 characters, and USER.md to roughly 1,375 characters. Operations must adhere to a strict taxonomic rule: Memory is for static facts; Skills are for dynamic procedures. If a documented workflow exceeds five distinct steps, the system is instructed to extract it into a dedicated [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|skill]] rather than polluting the bounded memory buffer. The primary [[Brand_Constitution/music/[[Brand_Constitution/music/[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/music/[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|identity]], overarching tone, and foundational [[DIRECTIVES|directives]] of the agent are established via a global, immutable SOUL.md file, which is loaded at the apex of the system prompt.   

Scheduled Operations via Cron Jobs

For an autonomous empire, repetitive tasks must execute without human initiation. The built-in /cron toolset allows the administrator to schedule recurring background jobs—such as executing nightly GitHub synchronizations, performing daily YouTube comment aggregation, or conducting hourly Procore RFI status checks.   

Unlike traditional Unix cron systems, Hermes cron jobs possess sophisticated time-bounded logic. An instruction such as "For the next 12 hours, check YouTube analytics every 10 minutes, then stop" is natively supported. The agent internalizes the scheduling logic, initiates isolated and sandboxed sessions for each execution, and autonomously kills the job precisely when the temporal window closes, ensuring no runaway processes drain API credits over the weekend.   

Token Economics and Multi-Model Routing

Operating a high-frequency autonomous agent can rapidly generate exorbitant API costs. The standard mitigation strategy for Hermes in 2026 involves separating the "reasoning" intelligence from the "utility" intelligence.   

Using a centralized routing provider like OpenRouter, the primary orchestrator can utilize a frontier model (such as Anthropic [[davinci-resolve-mcp/[[davinci-resolve-mcp/CLAUDE|CLAUDE]]|[[davinci-resolve-mcp/CLAUDE|Claude]] 3.5 Sonnet or OpenAI GPT-4o) exclusively for complex architectural planning and critical tool selection. Simultaneously, background cron jobs, routine data transformations, and high-volume text summarization tasks can be dynamically routed to high-performance, cost-effective open-weight models like Alibaba's Qwen 3.6 (27B/35B parameters) or DeepSeek V4. This architectural split, executed seamlessly via the /model custom:provider slash commands, has been demonstrated in production environments to reduce overall token expenditure by up to 90% without sacrificing reliability on mission-critical paths.   

Core Configuration File Taxonomy

The stability of the agent relies on the correct segregation of configuration data. Hermes enforces a strict hierarchy of precedence: Command-Line Arguments override config.yaml, which overrides .env, which overrides built-in defaults.   

File Path	Primary Function	Security Posture and Management
~/.hermes/config.yaml	Stores non-secret settings including terminal backends, MCP server definitions, toolset toggles, and model routing parameters.	

Safe to commit to version control. Supports dynamic runtime substitution via the ${VAR_NAME} syntax for variables. Modified via hermes config set. 


~/.hermes/.env	The exclusive repository for provider API keys, OAuth access tokens, and infrastructure passwords.	

Highly sensitive. Requires strict Unix permissions (chmod 600). Must never be tracked in version control. 


~/.hermes/skills/	The primary directory containing all agent-created and manually authored procedural [[.agents/skills/seo-geo/SKILL|SKILL]].md documents, alongside their supporting scripts.	

Automatically scanned by Tirith on load. Safe to share or synchronize across machines provided the logic is sanitized. 


~/.hermes/SOUL.md	The core system prompt defining the agent's [[Brand_Constitution/music/[[Brand_Constitution/music/[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/music/[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/possibilities/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|IDENTITY]]|[[Brand_Constitution/protocol/IDENTITY|identity]], operational tone, and foundational behavioral [[DIRECTIVES|directives]].	

Static configuration overlay. Acts as the permanent persona anchor across all sessions. 


~/.hermes/memories/	Persistent, dynamic storage for the FTS5-managed MEMORY.md and USER.md files.	

Dynamic runtime [[STATE|STATE]]|[[STATE|state]] continuously modified by the agent. Subject to strict bounded character limits to prevent context overflow. 

  

By formalizing procedural memory through the autonomous creation of Markdown skills and dynamically mapping external enterprise domains via FastMCP, the Hermes architecture transcends the limitations of traditional automation. The integrated learning loop ensures that as the business logic within the construction, media, and health verticals inevitably evolves, the agent autonomously patches its own operational files to maintain continuity. Supported by the rigorous security guarantees of container isolation and Tirith pre-execution scanning, this framework provides a highly secure, deterministically reliable, and endlessly scalable foundation for the Keystone Sovereign autonomous enterprise.
